body_txt: Something is released to the public, and something in it offends a group of people; say, for instance, members of a certain religion. They might call in to complain and express their distaste, but there's a twist: the creator of the work is also a devout member of that religion! Why would this happen? Differences in opinion and approach, typically. While people can often agree on harmful stereotypes, what constitutes a positive depiction may still be viewed as a stereotype . Maybe this practitioner has a particular social circle where this is acceptable and even an acknowledged in-joke, not realizing others would see it as a problem. Or maybe they did realize there would be a problem and plowed ahead because they felt an uncomfortable observation was needed . They can also be a genuine Boomerang Bigot . The point is that while works must be judged on their own qualities, the personal background of an author can serve to accuse of bias or hostility more complex to unravel. While this trope isn't limited to religious satire, it often finds a home here. Many critics will ask why the creator doesn't mock or criticize any other religion, and the reason is simple familiarity. The creator has intimate knowledge of their own faith from a lifetime of hearing its doctrines. This is doubly true if the other religion is a stark minority in their country. Fair for Its Day can also be at work, where something considered bold and progressive at the time it was released has gone to be seen as harmful, if not making such stereotypes iconic. Part of it can also be explained by a mixture of Occam's Razor and Hanlon's Razor . Offending the Creator's Own can come about because the creator just simply didn't think about how their work would be seen by others, and so remained oblivious to how it may look until after it's published for all to see. This is especially true if the creator lacks anyone that may be able to check their work and realize the accidental implications of it. Humans Are Flawed , after all, and even the greatest minds in history can make grievous blunders. On the other hand, it's also possible that the offended parties may not speak for the entire demographic, and may even constitute a Vocal Minority . Note that intentionally mocking your own group, particularly for comedic reasons, is Self-Deprecation (sometimes overlapping with N-Word Privileges ), a different trope. May result from Poe's Law . Compare Unwanted Assistance , Stop Being Stereotypical . The creator in question may be branded as a Category Traitor and/or Boomerang Bigot , wrongfully or otherwise. The inverse is Mexicans Love Speedy Gonzales , where a depiction of a group of people by an outsider that some might consider stereotypical or negative is nevertheless embraced by real members of that group. Compare My Country 'Tis of Thee That I Sting , where the creator is offending their own country on purpose. No examples outside the context of creators and media, please.
